analogy: Think of "Changing Cultural Traditions" through the chapter's own opening images: 106 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY THEME 5 Changing Cultural Traditions FROM the fourteenth to the end of the seventeenth century, towns were growing in many countries of Europe. Townspeople began to think of themselves as more ‘civilised’ than rural people. Towns – particularly Florence, Venice... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Changing Cultural Traditions is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 106 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY THEME 5 Changing Cultural Traditions FROM the fourteenth to the end of the seventeenth century, towns were growing in many countries of Europe.

The text explains that townspeople began to think of themselves as more ‘civilised’ than rural people.

The text explains that towns – particularly Florence, Venice and Rome – became centres of art and learning.

The text explains that artists and writers were patronised by the rich and the aristocratic.

The text explains that the invention of printing at the same time made books and prints available to many people, including those living in distant towns or countries.

The text explains that a sense of history also developed in Europe, and people contrasted their ‘modern’ world with the ‘ancient’ one of the Greeks and Romans.

The text explains that religion came to be seen as something which each individual should choose for himself.

The text explains that the church’s earth-centric belief was overturned by scientists who began to understand the solar system, and new geographical knowledge overturned the Europe- centric view that the Mediterranean Sea was the centre of the world.

The text explains that there is a vast amount of material on European history from the fourteenth century – documents, printed books, paintings, sculptures, buildings, textiles.

The text explains that much of this has been carefully preserved in archives, art galleries and museums in Europe and America.

The text explains that from the nineteenth century, historians used the term ‘Renaissance’ (literally, rebirth) to describe the cultural changes of this period.

The text explains that the historian who emphasised these most was a Swiss scholar – Jacob Burckhardt (1818–97) of the University of Basle in Switzerland.

The text explains that he was a student of the German historian Leopold von Ranke (1795–1886).

The text explains that ranke had taught him that the primary concern of the historian was to write about states and politics using papers and files of government departments.

The text explains that burckhardt was dissatisfied with these very limited goals that his master had set out for him.

The text explains that to him politics was not the be-all and end- all in history writing.

The text explains that therefore our wise and prudent ancestors... ordered that this definition of the public rule should go rather by the nobility of lineage, than by the estimation of wealth: yet with that temperature [proviso], that men of chief and supreme nobility should not have this rule alone (for that would rather have been the power of a few than a commonwealth) but also every other citizen whosoever not ignobly born: so that all which were noble by birth, or ennobled by virtue, did...obtain this right of government.’ G.

The text explains that consequently, in the minds of sceptics and non-believers, God began to be replaced by Nature as the source of creation.

The text explains that even those who retained their faith in God started talking about a distant God who does not directly regulate the act of living in the material world.

The text explains that an artist was not just a member of a guild, he was known for himself.

The text explains that in the eighteenth century, this sense of the individual would be expressed in a political form, in the belief that all individuals had equal political rights.

The text explains that another development was that the different regions of Europe started to have their separate sense of identity, based on language.

The text explains that europe, earlier united partly by the Roman Empire and later by Latin and Christianity, was now dissolving into states, each united by a common language.

The text explains that which elements of Greek and Roman culture were revived in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries?

The text explains that compare details of Italian architecture of this period with Islamic architecture.

The text explains that why were Italian towns the first to experience the ideas of humanism?

The text explains that compare the Venetian idea of good government with those in contemporary France.

The text explains that 122 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY ANSWER IN A SHORT ESSAY 5.

The text explains that write a careful account of how the world appeared different to seventeenth-century Europeans.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Themes in World History, Chapter 5: Changing Cultural Traditions

prelims_traps: Do not treat 106 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY and THEME as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Changing Cultural Traditions; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Changing Cultural Traditions through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 106 THEMES IN WORLD HISTORY and THEME to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
